text: The video displays a patient with a pantaloon hernia on the right side and a direct hernia on the left side.
The video depicts a laparoscopic bilateral inguinal hernia repair, where a single mesh is being used to address hernias on both sides.
According to the technique described by Stopper which provides for the positioning of a large mesh in the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon carefully dissects between the hernia sac and fascia transversalis, encountering tight and hard adhesions that make the separation challenging.
After reducing all hernias, the surgeon opens the suprapubic window to facilitate the passage of a large mesh into the preperitoneal space.
The surgeon uses a tailored macro porous partially absorbable mesh measuring fifteen by thirty centimeters, positioning it within the surgical field.
The surgeon shapes the large mesh into a pond-like form, preparing it for placement in the preperitoneal space during the laparoscopic bilateral inguinal hernia repair.
In this way it will be more difficult for the mesh to migrate.
The surgeon fixes the mesh bilaterally to Cooper's ligament using absorbable tacks, ensuring secure placement on both sides.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using an absorbable suture, completing the procedure.
